warning: AI-assisted monitoring. Verify against official legislation before product approval, export, label declaration, or customer compliance confirmation.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Additives DB (GSFA Online) | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth conditions for permitted food additives. Specific provisions are available in the searchable online database by food additive, functional class, and food category. Users are encouraged to consult the Preamble for interpretation.
rules.evidenceQuote: The "Codex General Standard for Food Additives" (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used in all foods...

rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: The Codex Alimentarius Commission establishes maximum levels (MLs) for contaminants in food or feed commodities, which are the maximum concentrations legally permitted to protect human health. The General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) is the primary reference.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Codex maximum level (ML) for a contaminant in a food or feed commodity is the maximum concentration of that substance recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission to be legally permitted in that commodity.

rules.limit: Adherence to Code of Practice required
rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: A Code of Practice (CXC 78-2017) exists for the prevention and reduction of mycotoxins in spices. This document provides guidance for minimizing contamination in this food category.
rules.evidenceQuote: CXC 78-2017 Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices

rules.sourceTitle: Database - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Food additives must be approved for use in the EU and comply with their conditions of use as listed in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008. Access to the Additives Database is required for verification.
rules.evidenceQuote: This database can serve as a tool to inform about the food additives approved for use in food in the EU and their conditions of use. It is based on the Union list of food additives. This list is in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

rules.parameter: Foodborne bacteria, toxins, and metabolites (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Food safety criteria define the acceptability of a product or a batch of food applicable to products placed on the market.
rules.evidenceQuote: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 on microbiological criteria for foods, applicable from 1 January 2006, lays down food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites, such as Salmonella , Listeria monocytogenes , Enterobacter sakazakii , staphylococcal enterotoxins and histamine in specific foods.

rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Shelf-life studies (durability studies and challenge tests) and sampling procedures for Listeria monocytogenes in food processing areas and equipment must be conducted according to technical guidance and guidelines to comply with food safety criteria in Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Technical guidance document on shelf-life studies for Listeria monocytogenes in RTE foods provides specialised laboratories with detailed and practical information on how to conduct shelf-life studies... The Guidelines on sampling the food processing areas and equipment describes sampling procedures...

rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Process hygiene criteria indicate the correct functioning of the production process.
rules.evidenceQuote: In addition, this Regulation lays down certain process hygiene criteria to indicate the correct functioning of the production process.

rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Special guarantees regarding Salmonella prevalence apply to trade of certain live animals and products from Sweden, Finland, and Norway. Other EU countries or regions can obtain similar guarantees if their control programmes are recognized as equivalent to those approved for Sweden and Finland, in accordance with Regulations (EC) No 853/2004 (foodstuffs) and 2160/2003 (animals).
rules.evidenceQuote: Special guarantees as regard Salmonella When Sweden and Finland joined the EU special guarantees on Salmonella were provided as regards trade from other countries of certain live animals and products... Other EU countries or region of EU countries can obtain special guarantees if it has a control programme recognised as equivalent...

rules.notes: This is a standard specification for optional shelf life periods.

rules.notes: This is a mandatory technical regulation for sports supplementary foods.

rules.notes: Verification of general requirements for Halal food is required for products claiming to be Halal.

rules.notes: Verification of specific microbiological limits for foodstuffs and food ingredients is required as per this technical regulation.
rules.evidenceQuote: This GSO technical regulation is concerned with microbiological limits for some foodstuffs intended for human consumption and for some food ingredients used in food industry.

rules.sourceTitle: Jaringan Dokumentasi dan Informasi Hukum Badan Pengawas Obat dan Makanan RI

rules.sourceTitle: Jaringan Dokumentasi dan Informasi Hukum Badan Pengawas Obat dan Makanan RI

rules.limit: Verification required against official specifications and standards.
rules.sourceTitle: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare: Food Safety Information
rules.notes: Specific limits and conditions for food additives are detailed in 'Specifications and Standards for Food, Food Additives, etc. Under The Food Sanitation Act'.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food Additives; Specifications and Standards for Food, Food Additives, etc. Under The Food Sanitation Act

rules.limit: Verification required against the Positive List System.
rules.sourceTitle: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare: Food Safety Information
rules.notes: The 'Positive List System for Agricultural Chemical Residues in Food' establishes maximum residue limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: Positive List System for Agricultural Chemical Residues in Food

rules.limit: Verification required against official specifications and standards.
rules.sourceTitle: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare: Food Safety Information
rules.notes: General contaminant limits are expected to be covered by 'Specifications and Standards for Food, Food Additives, etc. Under The Food Sanitation Act'.
rules.evidenceQuote: Specifications and Standards for Food, Food Additives, etc. Under The Food Sanitation Act

rules.limit: Verification required against guidelines for handling contaminated food.

rules.notes: Guidelines exist for 'Handling of food contaminated by radioactivity'.
rules.evidenceQuote: Handling of food contaminated by radioactivity

rules.limit: Verification required for specific nutrition labeling requirements.
rules.sourceTitle: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare: Food Safety Information
rules.notes: Requirements for nutrition labeling are outlined under 'Food with Health Claims, Food for Special Dietary Uses, and Nutrition Labeling'.
rules.evidenceQuote: Nutrition Labeling

rules.limit: Verification required against official specifications and standards.
rules.sourceTitle: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare: Food Safety Information
rules.notes: Microbiological standards for food are expected to be covered by 'Specifications and Standards for Food, Food Additives, etc. Under The Food Sanitation Act'.
rules.evidenceQuote: Specifications and Standards for Food, Food Additives, etc. Under The Food Sanitation Act

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Different limits apply based on the additive and food type; verification is required using SFA's Guidance Information on Requirements for Use of Food Additives.
rules.evidenceQuote: Different limits apply, depending on the additive and the type of food products. Use the Guidance Information on Requirements for Use of Food Additives to learn more about the different types of additives and the corresponding regulations that impose the limits.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Food additives are allowed if there is technological justification and they do not present health risks to consumers.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives are allowed when: there is a technological justification they do not present health risks to consumers

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Food additives must undergo safety assessment by SFA before being used in food sold in Singapore.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives must be assessed for safety by SFA before they can be used in food sold in Singapore.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply with the regulatory limits on food additives.
rules.evidenceQuote: All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply with the regulatory limits on food additives.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Only food additives assessed and permitted by SFA are allowed for use in food products.
rules.evidenceQuote: Only food additives that have been assessed by SFA are permitted for use in food products sold in Singapore.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Food products containing an additive that is not permitted must not be imported or sold.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food product contains an additive that is not permitted, do not import or sell it.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Food businesses must verify if additives are permitted using the SFA's List of Permitted Food Additives or Food Additives Search tool.
rules.evidenceQuote: Download the List of Permitted Food Additives or use the Food Additives Search tool to check if your additives are allowed.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Food businesses should be aware of commonly found contaminants in their products and conduct laboratory tests as needed.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food businesses must be aware of the contaminants that are commonly found in their food products and conduct laboratory tests as needed.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Food businesses must also comply with the Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food.
rules.evidenceQuote: In addition, they must also comply with the Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food .

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: If laboratory test results indicate contaminants, they must be within specified limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: If laboratory test results indicate that there are contaminants in your products, they must be within the specified limits .

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: All food businesses must comply with the regulatory limits on incidental constituents (contaminants) in food.
rules.evidenceQuote: All food businesses must comply with the regulatory limits on incidental constituents i.e. contaminants in food.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Contaminants (incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: Contaminants (also known as incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Verification of Singapore's regulatory limits for pesticide residues and Codex MRLs (if no local limits) is required.
rules.evidenceQuote: Pesticide residues Singapore's regulatory limits Codex MRLs (if there are no regulatory limits specified in the Food Regulations)

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Import or sale of meat and food derived from meat containing diethylstibestrol, hexoestrol, or dienoestrol is prohibited.
rules.evidenceQuote: Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations prohibits the import or sale of meat and any food derived from meat which contains residues of the following compounds: diethylstibestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-3-hexene) hexoestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-n-hexane) dienoestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-2, 4-hexadiene)

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Food products with contaminants exceeding regulatory limits must not be imported or sold.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food or food products contain contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed the regulatory limits, do not import or sell them.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Verification is required for specific limits on Microbiological standards, Heavy metals, Mycotoxins and Marine biotoxins, Veterinary drug residues, Pesticide residues, and Other incidental constituents from SFA documents.
rules.evidenceQuote: There are various limits imposed on different types of contaminants or incidental constituents in food. For more details, please refer to the relevant documents below: Microbiological standards... Heavy metals... Mycotoxins and Marine biotoxins... Veterinary drug residues... Pesticide residues... Other incidental constituents

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: SFA has established Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs) for veterinary drug residues in various food commodities, available on the SFA website.
rules.evidenceQuote: SFA has also assessed the risk of the presence of other veterinary drug residues in various food commodities, and has established a list of maximum residue limits (MRLs) for veterinary drug residues for compliance by food businesses with effect from 1 May 2020.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: For prepacked foods, all ingredients and additives used must be declared in the list of ingredients on food labels.
rules.evidenceQuote: All ingredients and additives used in food products must be declared in list of ingredients on food labels.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Ready-to-eat (RTE) food are not required to be tested for total plate count (TPC).
rules.evidenceQuote: Ready-to-eat (RTE) food are not required to be tested for total plate count (TPC).

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Verification of microbiological standards for ready-to-eat and non-ready-to-eat food is required from SFA documents.
rules.evidenceQuote: Microbiological standards Ready-to-eat food Non-ready-to-eat food (till 3 Mar 2024) Non-ready-to-eat food (from 4 Mar 2024)

rules.notes: Delisted color additives in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30) are not permitted.
rules.evidenceQuote: Delisted color additives in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30) (labeled as "DELISTED").

rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: Food additives and color additives must be listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173 and Parts 73, 74) for approved use.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives and color additives that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173 and Parts 73, 74 respectively)

rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: GRAS substances must be listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 182 and 184) or be subject to GRAS Notices.
rules.evidenceQuote: Generally Recognized as Safe ("GRAS") substances that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 182 and 184).

rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: Substances listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) are prohibited from use in food.
rules.evidenceQuote: Prohibited substances that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) as prohibited from use in food

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
rules.evidenceQuote: The major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.

rules.sourceTitle: List of Available Datasets
rules.notes: Specific limits for chemical contaminants can be found in the "Uncategorized Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool" and other relevant FDA resources.
rules.evidenceQuote: Uncategorized Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: If a food product is found to inadvertently contain a food allergen due to cross-contact, it is considered misbranded or adulterated and subject to enforcement actions.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA considers such products misbranded or adulterated, depending on the circumstances, and subject to enforcement actions such as recalls, import refusal, and seizure.

rules.limit: Must meet defined standard for gluten content
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The FDA has a final rule defining "gluten-free" for food labeling, including compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods.
rules.evidenceQuote: On August 2, 2013, the FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling, which helps consumers, especially those living with celiac disease, be confident that items labeled “gluten-free” meet a defined standard for gluten content.

rules.limit: Not required by law, must be truthful and not misleading
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Advisory statements like “may contain [allergen]” are not legally required and should only be used to address unavoidable cross-contact after good manufacturing practices have been incorporated. They must be truthful and not misleading.
rules.evidenceQuote: Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable “cross-contact,” only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes in their facility and have taken every precaution to avoid cross-contact

rules.parameter: Allergen preventive controls and accurate labeling
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The FDA's "Current Good Manufacturing Practice, Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food" rule (21 CFR part 117) requires facilities to implement allergen preventive controls to prevent cross-contact and ensure accurate allergen labeling.
rules.evidenceQuote: The CGMP & PC rule includes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Cochineal extract and carmine, derived from insects, must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products as allergenic substances.
rules.evidenceQuote: Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine, which are derived from insects, have been identified as allergenic substances that must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The food source of all major food allergens must be identified on packaged food labels. This can be done in parentheses following the ingredient name (e.g., "lecithin (soy)") or immediately after/next to the ingredient list in a "contains" statement (e.g., "Contains wheat, milk, and soy."). Specific types of tree nuts, fish, and Crustacean shellfish must be declared.
rules.evidenceQuote: FALCPA requires that foods or ingredients that contain a "major food allergen" be specifically labeled with the name of the allergen source. The allergen's food source must be declared at least once on the food label in one of two ways.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: All products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 must identify it on their labels due to potential hypersensitivity reactions.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA requires all products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 to identify it on their labels so consumers who are sensitive to the dye can avoid it.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Various sulfiting agents must be declared on food labels when present in food at a concentration of ≥10 parts per million total sulfites due to adverse reactions in sensitive individuals.
rules.evidenceQuote: they must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration in the food is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.

rules.sourceTitle: Hệ thống văn bản Chính phủ
rules.notes: Verification of specific additive limits for seasoning, snack food, and processed food products is required by searching the official legal document database.
rules.evidenceQuote: TÌM KIẾM VĂN BẢN
